title: Dye Transfer on Polyester
stainChemistry: Dye transfer occurs when loose dye from one garment migrates to polyester during washing — typically from dark denim, new colored items, or non-colorfast fabrics. The transferred dye bonds to the recipient fiber through the same mechanisms as intentional dyeing: hydrogen bonds, Van der Waals forces, and in some cases ionic bonds. Once set by heat or time, the dye behaves as if it were part of the fiber.
whyThisWorks: An oxygen-based bleach (for colorfast items) or reducing agent (sodium hydrosulfite for whites) breaks the chromophore bonds in the transferred dye on polyester. Speed is critical — fresh transfers respond dramatically better than set ones. Re-washing immediately in cold water with color-safe bleach is the most effective first response. Heat (dryer) makes dye transfer permanent.
defaultAssumption: Assume stain age is unknown and possibly heat-set unless user specifies otherwise. Start conservative.

steps: 1. Apply sodium hydrosulfite (reducing agent) to break the transferred dye bonds
2. Flush with NSD and cool water
3. If color remains: apply H2O2 6% as oxidizing bleach — 5-15 min dwell time, monitor carefully
4. Apply Acetic Acid 28% (diluted 1:10) as neutralizing rinse
5. Blot dry. Repeat full cycle if needed
warnings: Speed is critical — treat immediately before dye bonds

steps: 1. Re-wash IMMEDIATELY in cold water — do NOT dry
2. Dissolve 1 scoop OxiClean in cold water. Soak the item for 1-6 hours
3. Check progress every hour. Re-wash in cold water with detergent
4. Repeat soak if needed — some dye transfers require multiple cycles
5. Air dry only — dryer heat permanently sets dye transfer
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if the stain hasn't improved after 3 attempts. Take to a professional — they have specialized dye-stripping agents
warnings: Never rub dye stains — rubbing spreads the color

neverDo: No strong alkali at high temperature
fiberSensitivities: Oleophilic — grabs and holds oil stains tenaciously
Retains odors due to lipophilic surface
Most chemicals are safe on polyester

escalation.when: If the dye has penetrated the fiber (no improvement after 3 solvent passes), or if the fabric is expensive/delicate, or if dye continues to spread during treatment
escalation.whatToTell: Explain that the stain is dye transfer on polyester, how old it is, and what products were already used. This helps the professional choose the right chemistry without duplicating failed approaches

contextQuestions: Has it been exposed to heat (dryer, iron, hot water)?
Have you already tried treating it? If so, with what products?
Is the color still actively spreading or has it dried?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Stain age and heat exposure are the two biggest factors. Fresh stains with no heat exposure have the highest success rate. Heat-set or aged stains may require professional treatment.
